WA3850 Lab Guide

GitHub and GitHub Codebase Management for Leaders

Evergreen Quote Pull Request

Lab 1

1.1. Why This Lab Exists

Your lab Virtual Machine (VM) has a prepared starter version of the Evergreen Insurance quote page — HTML, CSS, Flexbox, a small JavaScript file. Today it is simply a small app in a folder on your laptop. Every change to it so far has lived entirely on your VM.

That is not how code gets into a real codebase. Every change typically enters a repository through a pull request or PR: an author proposes a set of edits on a branch, a reviewer reads the diff and leaves comments, Continuous Integration (CI) runs tests automatically, and only after approval does the change merge into main. You sit in PR-related conversations every day — standups, sprint reviews, post-incident retrospectives. Understanding what each part of a PR page means turns those conversations from vague to specific.

This lab walks you end-to-end through that cycle for the first time:

  • Take the Evergreen project and push it to a fresh GitHub repository of your own.

  • Create a short-lived branch with one small change — rewording the hero heading.

  • Open a pull request against main, fill out a real description (Summary, Why, Test plan), and self-review the diff.

  • Approve the PR and Squash and merge it.

  • Pull the merge back down to your local main and confirm the history is clean.

You may not be authoring pull requests as part of your day job after this week. You will be reading them — dozens per sprint. The muscle memory of having opened one yourself, even once, makes every future PR you read easier to parse.

 Tip

The pedagogical win this week is the workflow, not the change itself. The hero heading tweak is a placeholder for "any one-line edit." The ritual — branch, commit, push, open PR, describe, review, merge, pull back — is the sequence to recognize.

1.2. Open the Starter in VS Code

  1. Launch VS Code from the lab VM’s applications menu (or the desktop shortcut).

  2. In VS Code, choose File → Open Folder, navigate to Labfiles/evergreen-quote-pull-request in your home folder, and click Open.

  3. Open the VS Code integrated terminal via Terminal → New Terminal (or press Ctrl+` ). It opens at the bottom of the VS Code window, already pointed at the lab folder. Confirm with:

    pwd

    You should see a path ending in Labfiles/evergreen-quote-pull-request.

The project opens with three familiar files plus a README:

  • index.html — Evergreen page structure (provided for you)

  • styles.css — the stylesheet (provided for you)

  • app.js — the quote calculation script with the Auto rate set to 1.5 (provided for you)

1.3. Initialize a Git Repository

  1. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to confirm the folder’s Git status:

    git status

    If you see output starting with On branch main or similar — good, the folder is already a repository and you can skip ahead to Sign in to Your Provided GitHub Account.

  2. If instead you see fatal: not a git repository, in the VS Code terminal, execute the commands below to initialize one:

    git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial Evergreen quote page"

    The first command creates the hidden .git folder that holds all version history. The second stages every current file. The third records the first commit.

 Note

If git commit complains about a missing author identity, in the VS Code terminal, execute the commands below to set it once:

git config user.email "your.name@example.com"
git config user.name  "Your Name"

…​and re-run the commit.

  1. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to confirm the branch name is main (newer Git installs default to this; older ones may say master):

    git branch --show-current

  2. If it says master, in the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to rename the branch:

    git branch -M main

1.4. Sign in to Your Provided GitHub Account

  1. Open your browser (Firefox or Chrome — either works) and navigate to https://github.com.

  2. Sign in with your provided GitHub account credentials.

1.5. Create an Empty GitHub Repository

  1. Click the + icon in the top-right toolbar and choose New repository.

  2. Fill in the form:

    • Repository name: evergreen-quote-demo

    • Description: Practice repo for Phase 1, Week 2 — pull request walkthrough

    • Visibility: select Private.

  3. Click Create repository.

You land on an empty-repo page with a block of commands titled "…​or push an existing repository from the command line." That block is what you will run next.

1.6. Push the Local Repo to GitHub

GitHub shows two possible remote URLs — HTTPS and SSH. Use HTTPS for this lab (GitHub will prompt for a browser sign-in or a Personal Access Token the first time you push).

  1. Back in the VS Code terminal, from ~/Labfiles/evergreen-quote-pull-request, run:

    git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/evergreen-quote-demo.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main

    Replace YOUR-USERNAME with your actual GitHub username (the URL on the GitHub page has the right value; copy it verbatim).

  2. The first git push may prompt for GitHub credentials. Follow the prompts. If it opens a browser tab for you to click Authorize, do that and return to the VS Code terminal.

 Tip

If HTTPS authentication fails repeatedly, the easiest fallback is the gh CLI (pre-installed on your lab VM). In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below:

gh auth login

…​then re-run the git push. gh auth login handles credential storage for you.

  1. Refresh the GitHub page in your browser. The index.html, styles.css, app.js, and README.md files are now visible in the repo. The default branch is main and shows one commit.

1.7. Create a Feature Branch

  1. Back in the VS Code terminal, create and switch to a feature branch:

    git checkout -b update-hero-copy

    The branch name — update-hero-copy — describes the change. Many teams use naming conventions like feature/…​, bugfix/…​, or a ticket-tracker prefix. For this lab a simple descriptive name is fine.

  2. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to confirm the branch:

    git branch --show-current

    You see update-hero-copy.

1.8. Edit the Hero Heading

  1. Open index.html in VS Code. Around line 22, find the hero heading:

    <section class="hero">
  <h1>Coverage that grows with you</h1>
  <p class="tagline">Get a personalized quote in under a minute.</p>
</section>

  2. Change the <h1> text to:

    <section class="hero">
  <h1>Coverage that scales with your life</h1>
  <p class="tagline">Get a personalized quote in under a minute.</p>
</section>

  3. Save the file (use File → Save, or press Ctrl+S — if the shortcut is captured by the browser, the menu always works). If you have Live Server running, the page in the browser refreshes automatically and you see the new heading.

 Tip

This is a trivial text change on purpose. The copy tweak is a stand-in for "any one-line edit." The point this week is not the words on the page; it is the ritual that gets the words on the page.

1.9. Commit and Push the Feature Branch

  1. In the VS Code terminal, stage and commit:

    git add index.html
git commit -m "Update hero heading to 'scales with your life'"

  2. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to push the new branch to GitHub. The first push of a new branch needs the -u flag to set the upstream:

    git push -u origin update-hero-copy

    GitHub confirms the push and often prints a URL you can click to open the compare-and-pull-request page directly.

  3. Refresh the GitHub repo in your browser. A yellow banner and a green button appear at the top: "update-hero-copy had recent pushes — Compare & pull request."

1.10. Open the Pull Request

  1. Click Compare & pull request. You land on the Open-a-pull-request form. The base dropdown should say main; the compare dropdown should say update-hero-copy. Confirm both.

  2. Note that the title field is pre-filled with the commit message from your last commit. You can edit it if you want, but for this lab, leave it as-is.

    Update hero heading to 'scales with your life'

  3. For the body, write a real PR description — this is the single most valuable habit you can carry out of this lab. Use the following template:

    ## Summary

Rewords the hero heading on the Evergreen quote landing page from
"Coverage that grows with you" to "Coverage that scales with your
life."

## Why

The new copy reads as more active and connects the product to the
customer's life stage rather than implying a fixed growth trajectory.
Aligns with recent marketing guidance on voice.

## Test plan

- Opened `index.html` with Live Server locally; confirmed hero
  heading renders correctly at desktop and mobile widths.
- No JavaScript or CSS changes; the benefits row and the quote form
  continue to render unchanged.
- `git diff main..update-hero-copy` shows exactly one hunk (line 22
  of `index.html`).

    That is a real PR description. The Summary tells a reviewer what in one sentence. The Why tells them why — the single most important line in the description. The Test plan tells them how the author convinced themselves it works.

  4. Scroll down on the form. The Files changed section previews your diff. Green lines indicate additions, red lines indicate deletions. Spot-check that the diff is what you expect.

  5. Click Create pull request.

1.11. Self-Review the Pull Request

You land on the PR page proper. Take a moment to absorb the layout:

  • Tabs: Conversation, Commits, Checks, Files changed

  • Right sidebar: Reviewers, Assignees, Labels, Projects, Milestone

  • Bottom of the page: merge controls

    1. Click the Files changed tab. The diff renders in the standard two-color format (red for deletions, green for additions).

    2. Hover over any line in the diff. A blue + icon appears in the gutter. Click it. A comment box opens. Type a comment:

      Reviewed. Copy change looks good. No other changes in this PR.

    3. Submit the comment with Start a review, then go to the top-right corner and click Submit review. Three radio buttons appear:

  • Comment — no approval weight

  • Approve — counts toward branch protection’s approval requirement (disabled on your own PRs)

  • Request changes — blocks the merge

    1. Notice that Approve is greyed out with the message "Pull request authors can’t approve their own pull requests." That is a hard GitHub rule — it applies to every PR on every repo, regardless of branch protection. Select Comment, leave the comment field blank (or add a short note), and click Submit review.

  1. Switch back to the Conversation tab (if not already there). Your review now shows in the timeline as a comment. Because this repo has no branch protection requiring approvals, the merge button at the bottom is already green and ready to use.

 Note

On a real team, the PR author usually never approves their own PR. A teammate reviews the diff and clicks Approve, and branch protection rules typically require at least one such approval before the merge button unlocks. The mechanics you just practiced (opening Files changed, leaving an inline comment, submitting a review) are exactly what that teammate does — the only difference is which radio button they get to click at the end.

1.12. Squash and Merge

  1. Scroll to the merge controls at the bottom of the Conversation tab. Next to the green Merge pull request button is a small dropdown arrow. Click it.

  2. Three options appear:

    • Create a merge commit — preserves the branch’s commit history as a merge commit on main.

    • Squash and merge — flattens all the branch’s commits into one clean commit on main.

    • Rebase and merge — replays the branch’s commits on top of main one by one, no merge commit.

      Select Squash and merge. The button text changes. Click it.

  3. A confirmation box appears with the squash commit message (GitHub pre-fills it from the PR title). Leave it as-is or tweak it, then click Confirm squash and merge.

  4. The PR turns purple. A Delete branch button appears — click it. This is the button that saves every repository from accumulating dozens of stale branches over time.

1.13. Pull the Merge Back Down

  1. Back in the VS Code terminal, switch to the main branch and pull the latest:

    git checkout main
git pull

    The git checkout main switches your local clone back to the main branch (which, on your laptop, has not changed yet — it still reflects pre-merge state). git pull fetches the latest commits from GitHub and fast-forwards your local main to include the squash-merged change.

  2. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to confirm:

    git log --oneline

    You see two commits: the original "Initial Evergreen quote page" and a new "Update hero heading to 'scales with your life'" — the squash commit. The feature branch commits are not in the log because the squash flattened them.

  3. Open index.html in VS Code once more. The hero heading now reads "Coverage that scales with your life" — the merge is reflected in your working copy too.

  4. Your local feature branch still exists. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to clean it up:

    git branch -d update-hero-copy

1.14. What You Just Did

You just completed the full code-change ritual, end-to-end:

  1. Pushed a local project to a fresh GitHub repository.

  2. Branched off main for an isolated change.

  3. Committed the change on the branch, not on main.

  4. Pushed the branch to GitHub and opened a pull request.

  5. Wrote a real PR description with Summary, Why, and Test plan.

  6. Self-reviewed the diff in the Files changed tab.

  7. Left an inline comment.

  8. Submitted the self-review as a Comment (GitHub blocks authors from approving their own PRs).

  9. Squash-merged the PR into main and deleted the branch.

  10. Pulled the merge back down to your local main.

 Important

Every one of those steps happens dozens of times a week on any active codebase. The engineers you partner with will have the entire sequence as muscle memory. You do not need to have it as muscle memory — but you need to recognize every step when you see one. You now do.

1.15. Bonus: Open the PR from the gh CLI

Five extra minutes of practice, if you want them. The gh CLI is already installed on your lab VM.

  1. In the VS Code terminal, create and push a new branch with another trivial edit (change the <footer> copy, for example), then open a PR directly from the command line:

    git checkout -b footer-copy-tweak
# ... edit index.html (can be any trivial change), save ...
git add index.html
git commit -m "Tweak footer copy"
git push -u origin footer-copy-tweak
# note the use of gh and not git for the next step.
# run gh auth login if you haven't already authenticated with GitHub from the CLI.
gh pr create --fill

    gh pr create --fill uses your branch name and most recent commit message to fill in the PR title and body automatically. Good for scratch PRs; not a substitute for writing a real description when the change matters.

  2. Note the PR number at the end of the output. You will need it for the next step. Your number may differ from the one shown below. image::assets/the terminal output of gh pr create showing the new PR URL and number.png[The terminal output of gh pr create showing the new PR URL and number,800]

  3. In the VS Code terminal, execute the command below to close the PR from the gh CLI (replace <PR_NUMBER> with the actual number of the PR you just created):

    gh pr close <PR_NUMBER> --delete-branch

1.16. Review

You did one thing this week, and it stacks with everything before:

  • You took a project from your laptop to a real GitHub repository.

  • You opened a real pull request with a real description.

  • You self-reviewed a diff, left an inline comment, approved, and merged.

The next time an engineer says "can you review this PR?" — you know what to look at. The next time someone mentions "branch protection blocked the merge" — you know what that means. The next time a PR description says "Test plan: …​" — you recognize the template.

 Tip

Make it a habit to read one PR on a repo you care about every day for the next two weeks. You will be surprised how fast your fluency grows. A PR a day is ten hours a week of engineering context for the cost of ten minutes of reading.
